era_context: Late Bronze Age; the transition from Amenhotep III to Akhenaten in Egypt marks the beginning of the Amarna Period (c. 1353–1336 BCE, depending on chronological framework). The Hittite Empire under Šuppiluliuma I was at its height. Mycenaean Greece was in Late Helladic IIIA. The Shang Dynasty in China continued. This was an era of imperial consolidation, international diplomacy, and significant religious and political change in Egypt.

events.title: Death of Amenhotep III and succession of Amenhotep IV (Akhenaten)
events.description: Amenhotep III, one of Egypt's most prosperous and long-reigning pharaohs, died after a reign of approximately 38–39 years. His son, Amenhotep IV, succeeded him. Amenhotep IV would later change his name to Akhenaten and initiate a religious revolution, promoting the worship of the Aten (sun disk) over the traditional polytheistic pantheon. This succession marks the beginning of the Amarna Period, one of the most significant and controversial periods in Egyptian history. The transition may have involved a co-regency period, though this is debated among scholars.

sources.name: Temple inscriptions and stelae from Thebes and Malkata
Historiographic consensus on Amenhotep III's chronology and succession

events.certainty_note: Amenhotep III's death and Akhenaten's succession are well-attested, but the precise dating is debated. Chronological frameworks place this event c. 1370 BCE, with uncertainty of ±5 years. The nature and duration of any co-regency between Amenhotep III and Akhenaten are also contested.

events.title: Šuppiluliuma I's reign continues; possible regnal year 20–25
events.description: Šuppiluliuma I remained the Great King of the Hittites, maintaining control over Anatolia and Syria. The Hittite Empire was at its territorial peak. The succession of Akhenaten in Egypt may have prompted diplomatic adjustments, as the new pharaoh had different priorities and policies than his predecessor. Hittite-Egyptian relations would evolve during Akhenaten's reign.
key_figures: Šuppiluliuma I (Great King of the Hittites)

sources.name: Hittite royal annals (cuneiform tablets from Boğazköy)
Amarna Letters (later compilation, but referencing this period)

events.certainty_note: Šuppiluliuma I's reign is well-documented, but precise year-by-year dating is uncertain. Chronological frameworks vary by ±5–10 years.

events.title: Mycenaean Greece in Late Helladic IIIA period
events.description: Mycenaean Greece was in the Late Helladic IIIA period (c. 1390–1300 BCE), characterized by the development of palatial centers, Linear B writing, and extensive trade networks. The Mycenaean civilization was at a stage of cultural and economic development, with palaces at Mycenae, Pylos, Tiryns, and other sites. Trade with Egypt, the Hittite Empire, and the Levant was active, as evidenced by archaeological finds and later references in Egyptian and Hittite sources.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Mycenaean sites (pottery, architecture, Linear B tablets)
Later references in Egyptian and Hittite sources to 'Ahhiyawa' (Mycenaean Greece)

events.certainty_note: The Late Helladic IIIA period is well-established through archaeological evidence and radiocarbon dating. The dating of 1370 BCE is approximate within this broader period.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of Amenhotep III's death and Akhenaten's succession is contested among Egyptologists. Some scholars place this event in 1372 BCE, others in 1370 BCE, and still others in 1368 BCE or even later. The nature and duration of any co-regency between Amenhotep III and Akhenaten are also highly debated, with some scholars arguing for a long co-regency (10+ years) and others arguing for little or no co-regency. Additionally, the chronology of the Late Bronze Age remains one of the most contested issues in ancient history, with competing frameworks differing by up to 15 years. Some scholars question the significance attributed to Akhenaten's religious reforms, arguing that they were less revolutionary and more continuous with earlier religious developments than traditionally portrayed.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1370 BCE is heavily dependent on Egyptian royal inscriptions and administrative records, which are biased toward court-sponsored narratives. The Amarna Letters, a crucial source for this era, were compiled during Akhenaten's reign (c. 1353–1336 BCE) and do not directly document 1370 BCE. Hittite sources provide some independent perspective on international relations. Archaeological evidence (pottery, stratigraphy, radiocarbon dating) provides some independent verification but lacks year-level precision. The chronology of the Late Bronze Age remains contested, with competing frameworks differing by up to 15 years. Perspectives from smaller polities, peripheral regions, and non-elite populations are largely absent from surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Akhenaten's succession led to religious reforms and the Amarna Period
Akhenaten's succession occurred during Šuppiluliuma I's reign
Hittite Empire and Mycenaean Greece were contemporaries with active trade
